title: Quantum criticality from spectral collapse in the two-photon Rabi model
authors: Jiong Li, Jun-ling Wang, Qing-Hu Chen, Hai-Qing Lin
abstract: Spectral collapse in the two-photon quantum Rabi model (tpQRM) has long been regarded as incompatible with quantum criticality due to the absence of a vanishing excitation gap. We show that, in the anisotropic tpQRM, spectral collapse constitutes a genuine continuous quantum phase transition governed by a single soft mode. The excitation gap within the same parity closes as $ε_{sp} \sim |g - g_c|^{zν}$ with $zν= 1/2$, placing the system in the same universality class as the standard QRM, while the gap between different parities reflects symmetry-induced level splitting rather than a critical excitation. This soft mode defines a unique energy scale that controls both equilibrium and nonequilibrium properties, including macroscopic observables, quantum Fisher information, and Kibble-Zurek dynamics. These results establish spectral collapse as an experimentally accessible realization of quantum criticality in a few-body system and demonstrate that universality is fully determined by the soft-mode structure rather than by microscopic details.
summary: This paper resolves the long-standing debate regarding whether spectral collapse in the two-photon Rabi model represents a true quantum phase transition. By identifying a specific 'soft-mode' gap within the same parity sector, the authors demonstrate that the system follows universal scaling laws similar to the standard Rabi model. The study extends these findings to nonequilibrium regimes, showing that the Kibble-Zurek mechanism accurately describes the system's dynamics during a parameter quench. This establishes spectral collapse as a viable way to realize and study quantum criticality in few-body systems.
main_problem: Determining whether the 'spectral collapse' in the two-photon quantum Rabi model (tpQRM) constitutes a genuine continuous quantum phase transition and identifying the energy scale that governs its criticality.
main_result: The authors show that spectral collapse in the anisotropic tpQRM is a genuine continuous quantum phase transition governed by a single soft-mode energy gap within the same parity, placing it in the same universality class as the standard QRM.
method: The study employs the Adiabatic Approximation (AA), exact diagonalization, and perturbation theory, alongside the Kibble-Zurek mechanism to analyze nonequilibrium dynamics.
model_or_system: An anisotropic two-photon quantum Rabi model (tpQRM) consisting of a two-level system (qubit) coupled to a single quantized bosonic mode with $Z_4$ symmetry.
key_observables: Atomic polarization, photon number, Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), quadrature fluctuations, and residual energy following a quench.
important_parameters: Coupling strength (g), critical coupling (gc), anisotropy parameter (r), qubit frequency (delta), and quench time (tau_q).
main_assumptions: The Adiabatic Approximation is assumed to be asymptotically exact near the critical line; the system is initially prepared in the ground state for quench dynamics.
figures_summary: Figure 3 shows Wigner phase-space distributions of the ground state, including squeezed states; Figure 4 illustrates the universal Kibble-Zurek scaling of residual energy as a function of quench time.
paper_structure: The paper first defines the model and the problem of spectral collapse, then uses the Adiabatic Approximation to identify the soft-mode energy scale, followed by an analysis of equilibrium observables and universality, and concludes with an investigation of nonequilibrium dynamics via the Kibble-Zurek mechanism.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical bridge between few-body spectral collapse and many-body quantum criticality, offering an experimentally accessible platform for studying universal scaling and quantum phase transitions without needing the thermodynamic limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Energy spectra in the q = 1/4 subspace at r = 0.60 for (a) ∆= ∆c = 0.25 and (b) ∆= 1.0 (right). The horizon- tal axis is x = −log10(1 −g/gc). Open circles denote exact diagonalization results, and solid lines the adiabatic approxi- mation. Blue and red indicate states with positive and nega- tive parity, respectively.

FIG. 3. Wigner phase-space distributions W (x, p) of the ground state at r = 0.25, ∆= ∆c and g = 0.95gc. (a) The reduced W (x,p) obtained by tracing over the qubit. (b) W (x,p) conditioned on the qubit being in its lower state. Re- sults are computed with QuTiP [47].

FIG. 2. Log-log scaling of (a) the energy gap ǫsp and ǫdp, (b) photon number ⟨a†a⟩, (c) atomic polarization ⟨σx⟩, and (d) the quantum Fisher information FQ at ∆= ∆c for r = 0.25 (red) and r = 0.60 (blue). The corresponding scaling exponents for each observable are indicated.

FIG. 4. Residual energy Er as a function of the quench time τq, obtained from nearly adiabatic dynamics for ﬁnal cou- plings ranging from gf = 0.99gc to gf = (1−10−6)gc (bottom to top) at ∆= ∆c and r = 0.25. For gf = (1 −10−6)gc, the dynamics closely follow the universal Kibble-Zurek scal- ing (solid black line).
